blurb: One of the very first wildflowers of spring — silky lavender cups push up through the snow on fuzzy stems, then transform into shimmering plumed seedheads that persist for weeks.
spread: Clump-forming; non-spreading; taprooted and slow to establish — do not disturb
seasons: Silky lavender to pale purple cup flowers Mar–May (before or with new leaves); feathery silver-plume seedheads Apr–Jun; attractive ferny foliage through summer; foliage dies back by midsummer in heat
wildlife: Early-spring nectar and pollen for native bees, especially queen bumblebees; silky seedheads used by birds for nest lining
deer: Deer-resistant; toxic foliage is avoided by most browsers
toxic: ALL PARTS TOXIC — contains protoanemonin; causes digestive distress and mouth irritation if ingested; fresh sap causes skin inflammation and blistering. Handle with gloves. Keep away from children, pets, and livestock.

care.hardiness: USDA zones 3a–8b — native to the Colorado Rockies and High Plains and among the hardiest Front Range perennials. Established taproot clumps are reliably winter-hardy in Boulder's 5b–6a climate with no mulch or protection needed.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant container-grown transplants in spring (Apr–May) or early fall (Sep). Choose the permanent site carefully — pasque flower has a deep taproot and resents being moved once established. Well-drained to dry, south- or east-facing slopes mimic its native prairie and open-woods habitat. Work some coarse grit or gravel into heavy clay at planting to improve drainage. In containers, use a fast-draining alpine or cactus mix in an unglazed terra-cotta pot; the Front Range's intense sun and wind dry containers rapidly — check moisture every day or two in summer. Container roots are more vulnerable to freeze-thaw than in-ground roots; sink the pot or move it to an unheated but protected spot (garage, cold frame) through winter.[1]
care.sun: Full sun is ideal — 6+ hours of direct sun gives the best flowering and the compact, tidy growth habit. Some afternoon shade is tolerated in hotter, lower-elevation sites but can reduce bloom count. Avoid north-facing exposures.[1][2]
care.soil: Fast-draining, gritty, alkaline to neutral soil — gravelly loam, sandy clay, or rocky hillside soil. The Front Range's natural alkaline clay-loam works if drainage is good; amend with coarse sand or pea gravel on heavy sites. Never plant in waterlogged or constantly moist ground; root rot is the most common cause of failure. Lean, unamended soil produces the most floriferous, long-lived plants.[1][2]
care.water: Low to very low once established. Water newly planted transplants every 5–7 days through their first summer to anchor the taproot. From year two, natural rainfall (~15 in./yr in Boulder) is usually sufficient; water only during prolonged midsummer drought (every 2–3 weeks at most). The plant goes partially dormant in midsummer heat — do not interpret this as drought stress and over-water. Excess summer moisture is the quickest way to lose this plant.[1]
care.spacing: Plant 12–18 in. apart. Each clump grows slowly and does not spread by runners. Give it a well-defined, permanent spot and leave it undisturbed for years.[1]
care.sow: Sow fresh seed in fall (September–October) directly in the garden or in deep containers and leave outdoors to receive natural freeze-thaw cycles over winter. Germination occurs the following spring as temperatures warm. Do not sow in spring without prior cold stratification — spring-sown unstratified seed usually fails or germinates poorly.[1]
care.stratify: Seed requires cold-moist stratification if sowing indoors in spring: mix with damp sand or vermiculite, seal in a bag, and refrigerate until temperatures warm (at least 4–6 weeks). Seed viability drops quickly after harvest, so sow fresh or stratify immediately. Fall-sowing outdoors is easier and generally more successful than indoor stratification, allowing natural freeze-thaw cycles to break dormancy.[1]
care.depth: Sow at the soil surface or press seed very lightly into the top 0.1 in. — pasque flower seed needs light for best germination. Firm contact with moist soil is more important than depth.[1]
care.propagation: By seed: sow fresh seed in fall (easiest, most reliable) or cold-stratify and sow in early spring; seedlings are slow the first year and may not flower until year two or three. By division: possible but not preferred — divide mature clumps in early spring before flowering, making sure each division retains a substantial root section. Divisions are slow to re-establish. Root cuttings in late winter are also possible. Do not attempt division in summer when the plant is semi-dormant. Seed from established garden plants germinates freely if conditions are right.[1][2]
care.bloom: March through May on the Front Range — timing varies with elevation and snowpack, but pasque flower is typically the first or second perennial to bloom, often pushing through the last snow. Flowers open before or with the emerging leaves; silky plumed seedheads that follow are nearly as ornamental as the flowers.[1]
care.feeding: None. Lean, unamended soil produces longer-lived, more floriferous plants. A single light topdress of grit or coarse sand in fall is beneficial on clay-heavy sites; avoid nitrogen-rich fertilizer or compost.[1]
care.maintenance: Essentially hands-off once established. Deadheading is not necessary — the feathery seedheads are a key ornamental feature and allow self-seeding. Remove dead leaves in early spring before new growth emerges. Handle all plant parts with gloves (toxic sap). Do not divide or move established plants unless absolutely necessary.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Self-sows gently near established plants when conditions are right (well-drained, open ground); seedlings are slow and distinctive with their ferny, hairy leaves. Thin or transplant seedlings while very young (before the taproot anchors); mature seedlings cannot be moved successfully.[1]
care.troubles: Root rot from poor drainage or overwatering is the primary killer. Slugs may nibble new growth in wet springs; use iron phosphate bait if needed. Crown rot in heavy, wet soil — improve drainage rather than treat. Rabbits occasionally browse young growth; protect with a wire cage in the first season. Fresh sap causes skin irritation — wear gloves when weeding or dividing.[1][2]

edible.summary: Pasque flower is poisonous — the fresh plant and its sap are irritant and blistering, and it should never be eaten.[2]
edible.caution: All parts of fresh pasque flower (Pulsatilla) are toxic: the plant contains the irritant compound protoanemonin (released from ranunculin when the tissue is damaged), and NC State rates the poison severity HIGH. Fresh sap causes inflammation and blistering on skin contact (contact dermatitis), and ingestion causes severe pain in the mouth, vomiting, and diarrhea; it is also toxic to livestock. Drying reduces toxicity (the volatile protoanemonin breaks down), but never rely on this — wear gloves when handling, weeding, or dividing, and keep the plant away from children, pets, and grazing animals. Do not eat any part.[2]
